speaker: interviewer
text: And the date is December 7, 1995. Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of-- of myself in France while we were going through the French campaign. And they chose a-- a car that we captured from the German army, and which we appropriated for ourselves, for our military intelligence team. And it has the appropriate markings on it. And I'm studying a map.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of the Intelligence Center I was assigned to once the war was over in the summer of 1945, located in the town of Freising, Bavaria, approximately 25 miles north of Munich. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm not sure about the time frame on this. Can-- can-- can you hold it a second? This is a picture of my brother, Max Klein, dedicated to me in the city he lived in at the time and worked in, in Germany, Breslau, in 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: These two photos that come from a memorial that French Jews erected in Israel during the past decade. And it shows-- it's in the form of lists-- a long wall with lists of all the Jews deported from France, and the dates on which they were deported, and who were their guards, and giving the deportees' birth dates and places. And the right photo is a close-up showing my parents as-- in-- in--- about halfway down on that list, which happened to be August 19, 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are these letters about?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are letters from my parents that came to me. The one on the left-- yeah-- at that time they were still living in Waldorf, the town where I was born and grew up in. One month before their deportation to the south of France, to-- to camps in the south of France. This one on the left is dated September, 1940. In October 1940, they were deported on half an hour's notice. They had to assemble and get a few belongings together in-- in that rush. And sent to the south of France, where they were recorded in Camp de Gurs, which was a camp that had initially been built for Spanish Civil War refugees who had come across the border into France during the Spanish Civil War. And the envelope shows that it has-- had to pass German censorship, that it was opened by the high command of the German army. The letter on the right is the first letter I received from Camp de Gurs. And it's dated 6th of December, 1940. And in part, it comes-- this one-- this part is written by my mother, Alice Klein, and in part it reads, 'My beloved children, it is to be hoped that these lines will reach you soon, because they are meant to indicate that we are in the best of health. Unfortunately, we are still without any mail from you, aside from the cable you sent, something we just can't understand, considering how much mail already arrived from America. Today, I can report to you that father visited us yesterday, and thank God, looks and feels well. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As far as food is concerned, same thing. Each one provides for the other whatever he or she can obtain. Thank God, it's not too cold. But since yesterday, there are such torrential rains that one nearly perishes in the mud. Water and mud seep into our boots from the top, and we don't dare go outside unless absolutely necessary. The main topic of conversation here also concerns immigration, of course. I enclose an important document, which hopefully can go into the works immediately. It is not our wish, dear children, that you should spend all your money for us, because we know how hard you have to work for it. And at any rate, you will still need it once we'll be with you, if that be God's will.' And my mother goes on to describe what they are lacking. 'We neither have light clothes for either one of us nor do I have a blouse, suit or even a slip-on apron. The same goes for shoes, slippers, everything remained at home in the flush of excitement. Among the many thousands of people here, there are perhaps 100 who packed sensibly'. It shows how, even under the very worst of circumstances, my parents always managed to make light of the matter to some degree, so as not to worry us. They showed an incredible amount of faith and fortitude that-- that saw them through-- through most of this. And it-- it showed a-- a-- a-- a nobility of spirit in the face of the worst adversity imaginable. And it-- it-- it shows how-- how-- how some of these qualities came out when the chips were down. I'm immensely moved and proud that-- that they could maintain that attitude.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a flower that was contained in one of the 400 letters that I have from my parents for that period of time. In fact, ever since I-- I left my parental home. And this one, my mother was able to pick in a camp in the south of France and still thought enough of us to send us a flower, to-- to show that they still had a certain spirit and that they were still looking forward to-- to be saved. And, as I said, it's one of 400 such letters. Unfortunately, as we found out after the war, when we made inquiries to a tracing bureau with headquarters in Paris, France, in March of '46, we got this answer. 'In response to your letter of the 4th of March, 1946, we regret to inform you that Mr. Ludwig Klein and Mrs. Alice Klein, nee Nahm, were deported from Drancy on the 19th of August, 1942, in the direction of Auschwitz. And, to date, do not appear on our lists-- lists of re-patriots'. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is the actual document I just mentioned, which informed us of the fate of our parents. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait a minute. Wait. Are you ready?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the notice we received from the State Department in November of 1942, the time at which I was already in the American army, and unfortunately, two months after my parents had been sent to Auschwitz, as mentioned in the previous documents. It informs us that the State Department has now advised the Consulate in Marseille to issue visas to my parents. And it is the supreme irony of when it came. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I-- but I need to read it, unfortunately.

speaker: interviewer
text: Umm--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mmhmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an army document marked secret, which it was a G-2, in other words military intelligence periodic report, that apprised all concerned parties of the enemy situation. And it is one that is only possible once in any war, because somewhere down, it says, all German forces in Northwest Germany, Holland, and Denmark surrendered as of 0500 hours, May 6, I believe, 1945. And then it goes on to say, surrender of the entire German army-- armed forces is imminent.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, just-- just one minute, please. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is what has allowed us to rebuild our lives. It's a picture of our entire family, starting with the front row, with Jim and Lynn Klein's children, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jennifer, and Alexa. And in the second row, behind them, is Roger Simon, Julie Simon-- Leslie Simon is in the third row in the back, to the right of that. Then we have Lindsay Ullman, who are Jim and Vivian's daughter-- who is Jim and Vivian's daughter, then Melissa Simon, Leslie and Roger's daughter, Gerda, and Elissa, Vivian and Jim Ullman's daughter, and Andrew, her brother, and the woman in the red dress is-- is Lynn Klein, our son's wife, and he's standing behind her to the left. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the third row, on the left, is Jim Ullman, Vivian Ullman, then Leslie Simon, myself, Gerda, and the others I have already mentioned.
